title: Chocolate eclairs
subtitle: Choux pastry filled with cream and dipped in chocolate
excerpt: Chocolate eclairs made from choux pastry: piped fingers, baked hollow, filled with whipped vanilla cream, and dipped in a dark chocolate glaze. The shells must be completely dry before filling or they go soggy.
sourceNotes: The eclair (from the French for lightning, possibly referring to the speed with which they are eaten) is a French patisserie classic documented from the nineteenth century. Marie-Antoine Careme, in Patissier Royal Parisien (1815), describes choux-based pastries filled with cream; the specific elongated form filled with cream and glazed with fondant or chocolate appears in Parisian patisserie writing by the mid-nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), gives the form now standard.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake and dry the shells a day ahead; fill and glaze up to 4 hours before serving. Filled eclairs go soft overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180°C fan after 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked dough made by heating flour, butter, and water together then beating in eggs. The resulting paste is piped and baked: the high moisture content turns to steam in the oven, puffing the pastry up and creating hollow shells. Choux must be baked until completely dry or it collapses on cooling.

content.text: is overbake rather than underbake. Pull the shells out too soon and they collapse as they cool; baked until the shells sound hollow when tapped, they stay crisp for hours. The oven temperature drops from 200°C to 180°C after 15 minutes: the first blast of heat creates the rise, the lower temperature finishes drying the walls. After baking, pierce each shell immediately to let the steam out; trapped steam makes the shell soggy from the inside.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Combine the water, milk, butter, and salt in a medium saucepan over medium heat. Bring to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat and add the sifted flour all at once. Stir vigorously until the mixture forms a smooth ball that pulls away from the sides of the pan. Return to low heat and stir for 1 minute to dry the paste slightly. Transfer to a large bowl and leave to cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs one at a time with a wooden spoon or electric mixer, beating well after each addition. The finished paste should fall from a spoon in a slow, reluctant V-shape. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a 1.5 cm plain nozzle and pipe fingers about 12 cm long onto the lined trays, spaced 5 cm apart.

Bake for 15 minutes at 200°C fan, then reduce to 180°C fan and bake for a further 15 to 20 minutes until the shells are deep amber, firm, and sound hollow when tapped on the base. Remove from the oven and immediately pierce the end of each eclair with a skewer. Return to the switched-off oven with the door ajar for 10 minutes to finish drying. Cool completely on a rack before filling.

Whip the double cream with the icing sugar and vanilla to soft peaks. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a small plain nozzle. Push the nozzle into the pierced end of each eclair and pipe until the shell feels full. For the glaze, heat the cream until steaming, pour over the chopped chocolate, and stir until smooth. Dip the top of each filled eclair into the chocolate glaze and place on a rack. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to set the glaze before serving.
